77.title: Day 77 Assignment: SOLID & Clean Architecture
77.description: Strengthen architectural decision-making for solid & clean architecture with design review framing, trade-off defense, and implementation patterns.

questions.title: SOLID & Clean Architecture: Explain It Like an Interviewer
questions.question: You are whiteboarding **SOLID & Clean Architecture**. Explain the concept, where it breaks down at scale, and how you'd defend your design choices in an architecture review.
hints: Define the core terms in SOLID & Clean Architecture before giving examples.
Contrast a correct approach with a common but flawed approach.
Mention one runtime, scaling, or maintainability impact.
questions.solution:
## SRP in practice
A class should have **one reason to change** — typically one axis of business change.

## Example violation
`OrderService` that emails, bills, prints PDFs — any notification template change forces redeploy of billing logic.

## Scale failure
Teams step on each other; tests become slow and flaky.

## Fix direction
Split by **use case** classes or modules; inject collaborators.

## Failure mode after sloppy split
**Anemic domain** + **transaction script** scattered — still hard to test.

questions.title: SOLID & Clean Architecture Trade-offs Under Pressure
questions.question: Two architecture options both work for **SOLID & Clean Architecture**. Compare them for coupling, evolution cost, and operational complexity over 12 months.
hints: Compare behavior for normal case and failure case.
Discuss complexity and maintenance cost, not only speed.
Tie decision to team context and production constraints.
questions.solution:
## Clean Architecture trade-off
**Dependency rule** helps testability; cost is more interfaces/boilerplate.

## Incident angle
Poor boundaries mean a bug in PDF rendering takes down **payment** deployment.

## Failure mode
**Circular module dependencies** — "big ball of mud" with packages.

questions.title: SOLID & Clean Architecture Interview Scenario
questions.question:
In a design review, this challenge is raised for **SOLID & Clean Architecture**: Show SRP violation and refactor.

Explain your first version design, then evolve it for scale and failure handling.
hints: Start with symptoms and blast radius before proposing fixes.
Explain what you would measure to validate your decision.
Include rollback/degradation strategy for risky changes.
questions.solution:
## Review scenario
Reviewer asks: "Why does UserController call PaymentGateway directly?" — boundary violation.

## Response structure
Acknowledge leak → introduce **application use case** + ports; move I/O to adapters.

## Failure mode
**Framework annotations** in domain entities → untestable core.

questions.title: SOLID & Clean Architecture Coding Challenge
questions.question:
Implement this coding task for **SOLID & Clean Architecture**:

Show SRP violation and refactor.
hints: Start with a minimal correct implementation, then refine naming and edge handling.
Keep output deterministic and easy to verify.
For interviews, explain algorithm/architecture choice before code details.
questions.expectedOutput: multiple unrelated reasons to change in one class

questions.title: SOLID & Clean Architecture Follow-up Case
questions.question:
A stakeholder now asks for a major new requirement on top of your **SOLID & Clean Architecture** design. Which parts remain stable, and which would you refactor first?

Context: Show SRP violation and refactor.
hints: Name one non-obvious side effect of your first solution.
Separate short-term mitigation from long-term architecture change.
Include one rollback or guardrail.
questions.solution:
## New requirement: multi-channel notifications
Stable: **domain events** and **user entity**. Refactor first: introduce **NotificationPort** with adapters for email/SMS/push.

## Failure mode if you bolt on
**If/else** chains across the service → untestable and merge-heavy.

questions.title: SOLID & Clean Architecture Rapid-Fire Concepts
questions.question: For **SOLID & Clean Architecture**, define these in one line each: coupling, cohesion, consistency model, and failure domain. Then mention one anti-pattern.
hints: Keep each definition short and exact.
Use terms interviewers expect (not vague paraphrases).
End with one concrete anti-pattern.
questions.solution:
## Rapid fire
- **Coupling**: change ripple across modules.
- **Cohesion**: related behavior stays together.
- **Consistency**: transactional boundaries — often **per aggregate** in DDD.
- **Failure domain**: blast radius of a bug — bounded by module boundaries.

## Anti-pattern
**Shared DTO** types across all layers forever.

78.title: Day 78 Assignment: Design Patterns - Creational
78.description: Strengthen architectural decision-making for design patterns - creational with design review framing, trade-off defense, and implementation patterns.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Creational: Explain It Like an Interviewer
questions.question: You are whiteboarding **Design Patterns - Creational**. Explain the concept, where it breaks down at scale, and how you'd defend your design choices in an architecture review.
hints: Define the core terms in Design Patterns - Creational before giving examples.
Contrast a correct approach with a common but flawed approach.
Mention one runtime, scaling, or maintainability impact.
questions.solution:
## Singleton when it is ok
**Global config** or **expensive** client init (with caution) — still prefer Spring beans over hand-rolled singletons in modern apps.

## Danger
Hidden global **mutable state** under test breaks parallel tests.

## Failure mode
**Classloader** tricks in app servers create multiple singleton instances.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Creational Trade-offs Under Pressure
questions.question: Two architecture options both work for **Design Patterns - Creational**. Compare them for coupling, evolution cost, and operational complexity over 12 months.
hints: Compare behavior for normal case and failure case.
Discuss complexity and maintenance cost, not only speed.
Tie decision to team context and production constraints.
questions.solution:
## DCL vs enum singleton (Joshua Bloch)
Enum singleton is **serialization-safe** and **thread-safe** by design; DCL is error-prone.

## Trade-off
Enum less flexible for mocking unless designed with interfaces.

## Failure mode
**Partially constructed** instance visible without volatile (old DCL bug).

questions.title: Design Patterns - Creational Interview Scenario
questions.question:
In a design review, this challenge is raised for **Design Patterns - Creational**: Thread-safe singleton with DCL.

Explain your first version design, then evolve it for scale and failure handling.
hints: Start with symptoms and blast radius before proposing fixes.
Explain what you would measure to validate your decision.
Include rollback/degradation strategy for risky changes.
questions.solution:
## Interview trap
"Make this logger singleton thread-safe" — discuss **double-checked locking** + **volatile** or just use **static holder** idiom.

## Failure mode
**Synchronized** getInstance on every call kills hot path performance.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Creational Coding Challenge
questions.question:
Implement this coding task for **Design Patterns - Creational**:

Thread-safe singleton with DCL.
hints: Start with a minimal correct implementation, then refine naming and edge handling.
Keep output deterministic and easy to verify.
For interviews, explain algorithm/architecture choice before code details.
questions.expectedOutput: enum: serialization/thread safety; DCL: easy to get wrong

questions.title: Design Patterns - Creational Follow-up Case
questions.question:
A stakeholder now asks for a major new requirement on top of your **Design Patterns - Creational** design. Which parts remain stable, and which would you refactor first?

Context: Thread-safe singleton with DCL.
hints: Name one non-obvious side effect of your first solution.
Separate short-term mitigation from long-term architecture change.
Include one rollback or guardrail.
questions.solution:
## New requirement: multiple configs per tenant
Singleton **global** config breaks — move to **scoped** factory or registry with lifecycle.

## Failure mode
**Static caches** grow unbounded per tenant.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Creational Rapid-Fire Concepts
questions.question: For **Design Patterns - Creational**, define these in one line each: coupling, cohesion, consistency model, and failure domain. Then mention one anti-pattern.
hints: Keep each definition short and exact.
Use terms interviewers expect (not vague paraphrases).
End with one concrete anti-pattern.
questions.solution:
## Rapid fire
- **Coupling**: global singleton hides dependencies.
- **Cohesion**: factory families belong together.
- **Consistency**: config should reload coherently — avoid half-updated globals.
- **Failure domain**: bad singleton can take down whole JVM on init exception.

## Anti-pattern
**Singleton DatabaseConnection** without pool.

79.title: Day 79 Assignment: Design Patterns - Structural
79.description: Strengthen architectural decision-making for design patterns - structural with design review framing, trade-off defense, and implementation patterns.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Structural: Explain It Like an Interviewer
questions.question: You are whiteboarding **Design Patterns - Structural**. Explain the concept, where it breaks down at scale, and how you'd defend your design choices in an architecture review.
hints: Define the core terms in Design Patterns - Structural before giving examples.
Contrast a correct approach with a common but flawed approach.
Mention one runtime, scaling, or maintainability impact.
questions.solution:
## Decorator vs subclass
Decorator composes behavior at **runtime**; subclassing is compile-time extension.

## Example
Add **metrics** around `HttpClient` without changing call sites.

## Failure mode
**Stack overflow** of decorators if chained naively with deep stacks (rare but messy).

questions.title: Design Patterns - Structural Trade-offs Under Pressure
questions.question: Two architecture options both work for **Design Patterns - Structural**. Compare them for coupling, evolution cost, and operational complexity over 12 months.
hints: Compare behavior for normal case and failure case.
Discuss complexity and maintenance cost, not only speed.
Tie decision to team context and production constraints.
questions.solution:
## vs Proxy
Proxy often controls **access**; decorator adds **behavior**. Same structure, different intent.

## Failure mode
**Leaky abstraction** if decorator changes contract subtly.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Structural Interview Scenario
questions.question:
In a design review, this challenge is raised for **Design Patterns - Structural**: Decorator for text transformer chain.

Explain your first version design, then evolve it for scale and failure handling.
hints: Start with symptoms and blast radius before proposing fixes.
Explain what you would measure to validate your decision.
Include rollback/degradation strategy for risky changes.
questions.solution:
## Scenario
Add cross-cutting **retry** to payment client — decorator wraps core client; keeps **single responsibility** per layer.

## Failure mode
**Retry** on non-idempotent POST without **idempotency keys**.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Structural Coding Challenge
questions.question:
Implement this coding task for **Design Patterns - Structural**:

Decorator for text transformer chain.
hints: Start with a minimal correct implementation, then refine naming and edge handling.
Keep output deterministic and easy to verify.
For interviews, explain algorithm/architecture choice before code details.
questions.expectedOutput: decorator: add behavior; proxy: control access

questions.title: Design Patterns - Structural Follow-up Case
questions.question:
A stakeholder now asks for a major new requirement on top of your **Design Patterns - Structural** design. Which parts remain stable, and which would you refactor first?

Context: Decorator for text transformer chain.
hints: Name one non-obvious side effect of your first solution.
Separate short-term mitigation from long-term architecture change.
Include one rollback or guardrail.
questions.solution:
## New requirement: dynamic ordering of transforms
Decorator chain becomes rigid — consider **list of strategies** (composite) with explicit ordering config.

## Failure mode
Order-dependent transforms (hash then encrypt) silently wrong if reordered.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Structural Rapid-Fire Concepts
questions.question: For **Design Patterns - Structural**, define these in one line each: coupling, cohesion, consistency model, and failure domain. Then mention one anti-pattern.
hints: Keep each definition short and exact.
Use terms interviewers expect (not vague paraphrases).
End with one concrete anti-pattern.
questions.solution:
## Rapid fire
- **Coupling**: decorator stacks can couple layers if interfaces too wide.
- **Cohesion**: each decorator should do one thing.
- **Consistency**: ordering matters for crypto/metrics.
- **Failure domain**: retries multiply downstream load.

## Anti-pattern
**Mega-decorator** that logs, retries, metrics, caches in one class.

80.title: Day 80 Assignment: Design Patterns - Behavioral
80.description: Strengthen architectural decision-making for design patterns - behavioral with design review framing, trade-off defense, and implementation patterns.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Behavioral: Explain It Like an Interviewer
questions.question: You are whiteboarding **Design Patterns - Behavioral**. Explain the concept, where it breaks down at scale, and how you'd defend your design choices in an architecture review.
hints: Define the core terms in Design Patterns - Behavioral before giving examples.
Contrast a correct approach with a common but flawed approach.
Mention one runtime, scaling, or maintainability impact.
questions.solution:
## Strategy pattern
Encapsulate a family of algorithms behind an interface — choose at runtime.

## Example
**Tax rules** per country without giant switch growing forever.

## Failure mode
**Strategy explosion** — too many tiny classes without grouping.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Behavioral Trade-offs Under Pressure
questions.question: Two architecture options both work for **Design Patterns - Behavioral**. Compare them for coupling, evolution cost, and operational complexity over 12 months.
hints: Compare behavior for normal case and failure case.
Discuss complexity and maintenance cost, not only speed.
Tie decision to team context and production constraints.
questions.solution:
## vs Template Method
Template method fixes skeleton in base class; strategy composes behavior — favor strategy for **testability** and **composition**.

## Failure mode
**Context object** passed everywhere becomes a blob.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Behavioral Interview Scenario
questions.question:
In a design review, this challenge is raised for **Design Patterns - Behavioral**: Strategy selection by runtime context.

Explain your first version design, then evolve it for scale and failure handling.
hints: Start with symptoms and blast radius before proposing fixes.
Explain what you would measure to validate your decision.
Include rollback/degradation strategy for risky changes.
questions.solution:
## Scenario
Payment routing: **card vs wallet vs BNPL** — strategy selected by checkout type; failures isolated per strategy.

## Failure mode
**Partial failure** without compensation across strategies.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Behavioral Coding Challenge
questions.question:
Implement this coding task for **Design Patterns - Behavioral**:

Strategy selection by runtime context.
hints: Start with a minimal correct implementation, then refine naming and edge handling.
Keep output deterministic and easy to verify.
For interviews, explain algorithm/architecture choice before code details.
questions.solution:
## Strategy sketch
```java
interface Pay { Result pay(Order o); }
class Checkout { private final Map<Type, Pay> routes; }
```

## Failure mode
Missing **default** strategy → NPE at runtime.
questions.expectedOutput: strategy: open for extension; if-else: edit risk

questions.title: Design Patterns - Behavioral Follow-up Case
questions.question:
A stakeholder now asks for a major new requirement on top of your **Design Patterns - Behavioral** design. Which parts remain stable, and which would you refactor first?

Context: Strategy selection by runtime context.
hints: Name one non-obvious side effect of your first solution.
Separate short-term mitigation from long-term architecture change.
Include one rollback or guardrail.
questions.solution:
## New requirement: A/B pricing experiments
Strategy selection must be **deterministic per user** and **auditable** — inject **experiment resolver** with seeding.

## Failure mode
**Random** per request pricing breaks trust and caching.

questions.title: Design Patterns - Behavioral Rapid-Fire Concepts
questions.question: For **Design Patterns - Behavioral**, define these in one line each: coupling, cohesion, consistency model, and failure domain. Then mention one anti-pattern.
hints: Keep each definition short and exact.
Use terms interviewers expect (not vague paraphrases).
End with one concrete anti-pattern.
questions.solution:
## Rapid fire
- **Coupling**: strategy interface too wide couples all implementations.
- **Cohesion**: keep strategies small.
- **Consistency**: choose strategy before transactional boundary.
- **Failure domain**: mis-routed strategy charges wrong amount — needs compensating transaction.

## Anti-pattern
**God switch** in one method with 40 cases.

81.title: Day 81 Assignment: System Design Fundamentals
81.description: Strengthen architectural decision-making for system design fundamentals with design review framing, trade-off defense, and implementation patterns.

questions.title: System Design Fundamentals: Explain It Like an Interviewer
questions.question: You are whiteboarding **System Design Fundamentals**. Explain the concept, where it breaks down at scale, and how you'd defend your design choices in an architecture review.
hints: Define the core terms in System Design Fundamentals before giving examples.
Contrast a correct approach with a common but flawed approach.
Mention one runtime, scaling, or maintainability impact.
questions.solution:
## Rate limiting goals
Protect **dependencies**, ensure **fairness**, and provide predictable **latency**.

## Example
100 req/min/user → token bucket with **burst** allowance for interactive UX.

## Failure mode
**Global limit** only — one noisy tenant affects everyone.

questions.title: System Design Fundamentals Trade-offs Under Pressure
questions.question: Two architecture options both work for **System Design Fundamentals**. Compare them for coupling, evolution cost, and operational complexity over 12 months.
hints: Compare behavior for normal case and failure case.
Discuss complexity and maintenance cost, not only speed.
Tie decision to team context and production constraints.
questions.solution:
## Algorithms
Token bucket (smooth + burst), leaky bucket (smooth output), sliding window (accuracy).

## Trade-off
Accuracy vs memory — sliding window log is heavier than counters.

## Failure mode
**Distributed limiter** without sync → double budget across nodes.

questions.title: System Design Fundamentals Interview Scenario
questions.question:
In a design review, this challenge is raised for **System Design Fundamentals**: Rate limiter for 100 req/user/min.

Explain your first version design, then evolve it for scale and failure handling.
hints: Start with symptoms and blast radius before proposing fixes.
Explain what you would measure to validate your decision.
Include rollback/degradation strategy for risky changes.
questions.solution:
## Scenario
Spike from marketing campaign — return **429** with **Retry-After**, shed load at edge, protect DB.

## Failure mode
**Retry storm** from clients without jitter.

questions.title: System Design Fundamentals Coding Challenge
questions.question:
Implement this coding task for **System Design Fundamentals**:

Rate limiter for 100 req/user/min.
hints: Start with a minimal correct implementation, then refine naming and edge handling.
Keep output deterministic and easy to verify.
For interviews, explain algorithm/architecture choice before code details.
questions.expectedOutput: edge: cheap rejection; app: domain-aware limits

questions.title: System Design Fundamentals Follow-up Case
questions.question:
A stakeholder now asks for a major new requirement on top of your **System Design Fundamentals** design. Which parts remain stable, and which would you refactor first?

Context: Rate limiter for 100 req/user/min.
hints: Name one non-obvious side effect of your first solution.
Separate short-term mitigation from long-term architecture change.
Include one rollback or guardrail.
questions.solution:
## After raising limits
Latency improved but **cost** spiked — downstream SaaS per-call billing.

## Fix
Tiered limits + **cache** for expensive operations.

## Failure mode
**Thundering herd** on cache expiry.

questions.title: System Design Fundamentals Rapid-Fire Concepts
questions.question: For **System Design Fundamentals**, define these in one line each: coupling, cohesion, consistency model, and failure domain. Then mention one anti-pattern.
hints: Keep each definition short and exact.
Use terms interviewers expect (not vague paraphrases).
End with one concrete anti-pattern.
questions.solution:
## Rapid fire
- **Coupling**: limiter keys tied to tenant/user/device — choose carefully.
- **Cohesion**: keep enforcement at **one** layer or coordinate explicitly.
- **Consistency**: global counts need **Redis** / gossip coordination.
- **Failure domain**: limiter outage should **fail closed** or **open** by policy.

## Anti-pattern
Rate limit **after** expensive work already done.

82.title: Day 82 Assignment: System Design - URL Shortener
82.description: Strengthen architectural decision-making for system design - url shortener with design review framing, trade-off defense, and implementation patterns.

questions.title: System Design - URL Shortener: Explain It Like an Interviewer
questions.question: You are whiteboarding **System Design - URL Shortener**. Explain the concept, where it breaks down at scale, and how you'd defend your design choices in an architecture review.
hints: Define the core terms in System Design - URL Shortener before giving examples.
Contrast a correct approach with a common but flawed approach.
Mention one runtime, scaling, or maintainability impact.
questions.solution:
## URL shortener core
**Redirect lookup** by short key → long URL; **write path** creates mapping; must be **low latency** reads.

## Example
Base62 encoding of auto-increment or random tokens with collision checks.

## Failure mode
**Predictable** keys enable scraping/guessing — use **entropy** + abuse protection.

questions.title: System Design - URL Shortener Trade-offs Under Pressure
questions.question: Two architecture options both work for **System Design - URL Shortener**. Compare them for coupling, evolution cost, and operational complexity over 12 months.
hints: Compare behavior for normal case and failure case.
Discuss complexity and maintenance cost, not only speed.
Tie decision to team context and production constraints.
questions.solution:
## SQL vs NoSQL
SQL with PK index works; NoSQL with partition key = short code scales horizontally.

## Trade-off
**Hot keys** if sequential IDs expose patterns; shard by prefix.

## Failure mode
**Dual writes** to cache and DB without TTL coherence.

questions.title: System Design - URL Shortener Interview Scenario
questions.question:
In a design review, this challenge is raised for **System Design - URL Shortener**: Base62 key generation and storage design.

Explain your first version design, then evolve it for scale and failure handling.
hints: Start with symptoms and blast radius before proposing fixes.
Explain what you would measure to validate your decision.
Include rollback/degradation strategy for risky changes.
questions.solution:
## Scenario
Spike from viral link — **CDN/edge cache** for redirects, **rate limit** create endpoint.

## Failure mode
**Cache stampede** on hot URL expiration.

questions.title: System Design - URL Shortener Coding Challenge
questions.question:
Implement this coding task for **System Design - URL Shortener**:

Base62 key generation and storage design.
hints: Start with a minimal correct implementation, then refine naming and edge handling.
Keep output deterministic and easy to verify.
For interviews, explain algorithm/architecture choice before code details.
questions.solution:
## Schema sketch
```text
short_code (PK), long_url, created_at, owner_id, expires_at
```

## Base62
Map 0-61 to [a-zA-Z0-9] — avoid ambiguous chars if product requires.

## Failure mode
**Collision** on random generation without retry loop.

questions.title: System Design - URL Shortener Follow-up Case
questions.question:
A stakeholder now asks for a major new requirement on top of your **System Design - URL Shortener** design. Which parts remain stable, and which would you refactor first?

Context: Base62 key generation and storage design.
hints: Name one non-obvious side effect of your first solution.
Separate short-term mitigation from long-term architecture change.
Include one rollback or guardrail.
questions.solution:
## New requirement: editable long URLs
Stable: **short code** immutable; add **version** or new mapping with **redirect type** (301 vs 302) policy.

## Failure mode
**301 caches** forever at browsers — user stuck on old destination.

questions.title: System Design - URL Shortener Rapid-Fire Concepts
questions.question: For **System Design - URL Shortener**, define these in one line each: coupling, cohesion, consistency model, and failure domain. Then mention one anti-pattern.
hints: Keep each definition short and exact.
Use terms interviewers expect (not vague paraphrases).
End with one concrete anti-pattern.
questions.solution:
## Rapid fire
- **Coupling**: analytics pipeline tied to redirect path — isolate async.
- **Cohesion**: create/read APIs separate scaling profiles.
- **Consistency**: read-after-write for creator UI — use **strong** read or token.
- **Failure domain**: abuse uploads — malware scanning on create.

## Anti-pattern
Storing **only** hashed URL without canonicalization → duplicates.

83.title: Day 83 Assignment: System Design - Notification Service
83.description: Strengthen architectural decision-making for system design - notification service with design review framing, trade-off defense, and implementation patterns.

questions.title: System Design - Notification Service: Explain It Like an Interviewer
questions.question: You are whiteboarding **System Design - Notification Service**. Explain the concept, where it breaks down at scale, and how you'd defend your design choices in an architecture review.
hints: Define the core terms in System Design - Notification Service before giving examples.
Contrast a correct approach with a common but flawed approach.
Mention one runtime, scaling, or maintainability impact.
questions.solution:
## Notification service
Fan-out events to channels (push/email/SMS) with **provider adapters** and **per-user preferences**.

## Example
Queue per channel with different **SLA** and **retry** policies.

## Failure mode
**Fan-out storm** from celebrity mention — need **rate limiting** + **sharding**.

questions.title: System Design - Notification Service Trade-offs Under Pressure
questions.question: Two architecture options both work for **System Design - Notification Service**. Compare them for coupling, evolution cost, and operational complexity over 12 months.
hints: Compare behavior for normal case and failure case.
Discuss complexity and maintenance cost, not only speed.
Tie decision to team context and production constraints.
questions.solution:
## Push vs pull
Mobile push is provider-mediated; email is SMTP batch; pull models for web inbox.

## Trade-off
Push is fast but **opaque** delivery guarantees; email has **retries** and **bounces**.

## Failure mode
**Duplicate notifications** without idempotency keys per event.

questions.title: System Design - Notification Service Interview Scenario
questions.question:
In a design review, this challenge is raised for **System Design - Notification Service**: Scale to 10M notifications/day.

Explain your first version design, then evolve it for scale and failure handling.
hints: Start with symptoms and blast radius before proposing fixes.
Explain what you would measure to validate your decision.
Include rollback/degradation strategy for risky changes.
questions.solution:
## Scenario
10M/day → partition by **user_id**, **autoscale** workers, **dead letter** for provider 4xx/5xx separately.

## Failure mode
**Poison** provider credential rotates → all channels fail if shared client poorly isolated.

questions.title: System Design - Notification Service Coding Challenge
questions.question:
Implement this coding task for **System Design - Notification Service**:

Scale to 10M notifications/day.
hints: Start with a minimal correct implementation, then refine naming and edge handling.
Keep output deterministic and easy to verify.
For interviews, explain algorithm/architecture choice before code details.
questions.expectedOutput: different retry, cost, and SLA per channel

questions.title: System Design - Notification Service Follow-up Case
questions.question:
A stakeholder now asks for a major new requirement on top of your **System Design - Notification Service** design. Which parts remain stable, and which would you refactor first?

Context: Scale to 10M notifications/day.
hints: Name one non-obvious side effect of your first solution.
Separate short-term mitigation from long-term architecture change.
Include one rollback or guardrail.
questions.solution:
## New requirement: user-defined quiet hours
Add **schedule table** + **timezone** correctness — DST bugs cause midnight spam.

## Failure mode
**Cron in UTC** only — wrong local quiet hours.

questions.title: System Design - Notification Service Rapid-Fire Concepts
questions.question: For **System Design - Notification Service**, define these in one line each: coupling, cohesion, consistency model, and failure domain. Then mention one anti-pattern.
hints: Keep each definition short and exact.
Use terms interviewers expect (not vague paraphrases).
End with one concrete anti-pattern.
questions.solution:
## Rapid fire
- **Coupling**: templating engine coupled to provider — use adapter.
- **Cohesion**: batch vs transactional notifications separate.
- **Consistency**: at-least-once job queue + idempotent send.
- **Failure domain**: provider outage — degrade gracefully (banner in app).

## Anti-pattern
**Infinite retry** on 400-class errors.

84.title: Day 84 Assignment: System Design - E-commerce Order Service
84.description: Strengthen architectural decision-making for system design - e-commerce order service with design review framing, trade-off defense, and implementation patterns.

questions.title: System Design - E-commerce Order Service: Explain It Like an Interviewer
questions.question: You are whiteboarding **System Design - E-commerce Order Service**. Explain the concept, where it breaks down at scale, and how you'd defend your design choices in an architecture review.
hints: Define the core terms in System Design - E-commerce Order Service before giving examples.
Contrast a correct approach with a common but flawed approach.
Mention one runtime, scaling, or maintainability impact.
questions.solution:
## Order service
State machine: Created → Paid → Fulfilled / Cancelled; integrate **inventory**, **payment**, **shipping**.

## Example
Saga orchestration with **timeouts** and **compensation** messages.

## Failure mode
**Lost message** between steps → stuck order — need **reconciliation** job.

questions.title: System Design - E-commerce Order Service Trade-offs Under Pressure
questions.question: Two architecture options both work for **System Design - E-commerce Order Service**. Compare them for coupling, evolution cost, and operational complexity over 12 months.
hints: Compare behavior for normal case and failure case.
Discuss complexity and maintenance cost, not only speed.
Tie decision to team context and production constraints.
questions.solution:
## CQRS touch
Commands mutate state; queries read **projections** — trade complexity for scale.

## Failure mode
**Projection lag** shows stale "shipped" in UI — define **staleness** SLA.

questions.title: System Design - E-commerce Order Service Interview Scenario
questions.question:
In a design review, this challenge is raised for **System Design - E-commerce Order Service**: Order state machine with saga/CQRS hooks.

Explain your first version design, then evolve it for scale and failure handling.
hints: Start with symptoms and blast radius before proposing fixes.
Explain what you would measure to validate your decision.
Include rollback/degradation strategy for risky changes.
questions.solution:
## Scenario
Payment succeeds, inventory fails — **compensating refund** saga step; must be **idempotent**.

## Failure mode
**Double refund** if compensation retried.

questions.title: System Design - E-commerce Order Service Coding Challenge
questions.question:
Implement this coding task for **System Design - E-commerce Order Service**:

Order state machine with saga/CQRS hooks.
hints: Start with a minimal correct implementation, then refine naming and edge handling.
Keep output deterministic and easy to verify.
For interviews, explain algorithm/architecture choice before code details.
questions.expectedOutput: saga: loose coupling; 2PC: strong consistency, not always available

questions.title: System Design - E-commerce Order Service Follow-up Case
questions.question:
A stakeholder now asks for a major new requirement on top of your **System Design - E-commerce Order Service** design. Which parts remain stable, and which would you refactor first?

Context: Order state machine with saga/CQRS hooks.
hints: Name one non-obvious side effect of your first solution.
Separate short-term mitigation from long-term architecture change.
Include one rollback or guardrail.
questions.solution:
## New requirement: partial shipments
Stable: **order aggregate**; add **shipment** sub-entity with its own state — avoid boolean flags explosion.

## Failure mode
**Monolithic state enum** becomes unmaintainable.

questions.title: System Design - E-commerce Order Service Rapid-Fire Concepts
questions.question: For **System Design - E-commerce Order Service**, define these in one line each: coupling, cohesion, consistency model, and failure domain. Then mention one anti-pattern.
hints: Keep each definition short and exact.
Use terms interviewers expect (not vague paraphrases).
End with one concrete anti-pattern.
questions.solution:
## Rapid fire
- **Coupling**: payment provider webhooks tightly coupled to core — use **adapter**.
- **Cohesion**: keep saga orchestration in one module with explicit steps.
- **Consistency**: define **per step** consistency (strong vs eventual).
- **Failure domain**: inventory oversell — **optimistic locking** + reconciliation.

## Anti-pattern
**Synchronous** chain of HTTP calls with no timeouts across services.
